generated_note:
**Subjective**
**Chief Complaint**
Anxiety

**History of Present Illness**
Mr. Nakamura is a gentleman coming in for a new complaint of anxiety. He was actually brought in largely at the urging of his wife, who's been encouraging him to seek help for months. The presenting example that really crystallized things for him was last week at his seven-year-old daughter's school play — he was sitting in the audience and found himself mentally running through work spreadsheets instead of watching his daughter perform on stage. His wife noticed, told him this wasn't normal, and he agreed to come in.

He has been experiencing significant anxiety for approximately a year and a half to two years, but the last six months have been notably worse. The worry has generalized well beyond work. He worries about his kids' health, finances — he noted they're actually financially stable — whether he left the stove on, whether he said something wrong to someone at a party weeks prior. He describes it as a radio he can't turn off. It's present most of the day, starting within about ten minutes of waking up. He's tried to redirect his attention but the worry slides back in. Importantly, he recognizes that most of his worries are irrational, but he cannot control them — the logic doesn't help.

Physical symptoms are significant. He carries a lot of muscle tension — shoulders, neck, trapezius area. His wife reports he grinds his teeth at night, and he actually cracked a molar last year and was fitted with a night guard by his dentist. He gets tension headaches three to four times a week. GI symptoms include a constant low-grade nausea — not emesis, just an unsettled feeling — and looser stools, not frank diarrhea but not normal. He saw a GI doctor six months ago, had both an endoscopy and colonoscopy, both of which were normal, and was told it was likely irritable bowel syndrome and advised to increase fiber intake. He noted that his GI symptoms are worst on Monday mornings, which correlates with his dread of going into work.

Sleep is significantly impaired. He has difficulty falling asleep — takes forty-five minutes to an hour most nights — due to racing thoughts about what could go wrong tomorrow, next week, next year. He's started listening to podcasts to drown out the thoughts, which ends up keeping him up even later. He mostly stays asleep once he's out, but wakes feeling unrestored. He describes his daytime state as "tired but wired" — exhausted but unable to relax, like he's had ten cups of coffee.

Cardiovascular symptoms — he does experience occasional palpitations when highly anxious. He had an episode during a work presentation last month where he felt his heart thumping and briefly thought he might be having a heart attack, but it resolved within about fifteen seconds and did not escalate into a full panic attack. He denies true panic attacks. He has mild hand tremor, particularly noticeable in meetings — he's been hiding his hands under the table. He has occasional shortness of breath, noting he sometimes catches himself taking shallow breaths and has to consciously take a deep breath. Mild palmar diaphoresis in stressful situations. Mild orthostatic lightheadedness, nothing significant. He is also extremely irritable — snaps at his kids, feels guilty afterward. His wife has told him she feels like she's walking on eggshells.

Social history — caffeine is one cup of coffee in the morning; he previously drank three cups and cut back on his own after reading it could worsen anxiety. Alcohol is one to two beers in the evening occasionally, which he uses to quiet his mind. He has noticed that mornings after drinking are worse — he acknowledged this when I mentioned the concept of rebound anxiety. No recreational drugs, no tobacco.

He has no prior psychiatric treatment — never been to therapy, never been on medication. He grew up in a family where mental health wasn't discussed and he was expected to just manage. Family history is notable — his mother was described as always a worrier, no formal diagnosis known. His older brother is on escitalopram for anxiety and reportedly does well on it.

Medical history — he had a checkup six months ago with reportedly normal blood work, though he was uncertain whether his thyroid was checked. No known thyroid disease, no cardiac history.

**Past Medical History**
Checkup six months ago with reportedly normal blood work, though he was uncertain whether his thyroid was checked. No known thyroid disease, no cardiac history.

**Physical Examination**
Significant bilateral trapezius muscle tension on palpation.
Cardiac exam: Regular rate and rhythm, no murmurs.
Lungs: Clear.
Thyroid: Normal to palpation.
Neurological exam: Grossly normal.

**Assessment**
Mr. Nakamura meets full criteria for generalized anxiety disorder. He has persistent, uncontrollable worry across multiple life domains lasting well over six months, accompanied by muscle tension, sleep disturbance, GI symptoms, fatigue, difficulty concentrating, irritability, and associated physical symptoms. This is significantly impacting his work performance, his marriage, and his relationship with his children.

**Plan**
1.  Labs ordered including TSH, CBC, CMP.
2.  Discussed evidence base for combined psychotherapy and pharmacotherapy. Explained CBT as gold standard for GAD, ACT as alternative evidence-based approach. He expressed openness to therapy and I placed referral to behavioral health.
3.  Discussed SSRIs, SNRIs, buspirone. Given severity of symptoms (GAD-7=17, significant functional impairment) and family history of brother responding well to escitalopram, prescribed escitalopram. Starting at 5mg daily for first week, then increase to 10mg. Counselled on taking 2-4 weeks to begin working, 6-8 weeks for full effect. Reviewed common side effects (initial nausea, headache, possible transient increase in anxiety, potential for longer-term sexual side effects including decreased libido and difficulty with orgasm). Counselled on when to call (startup side effects severe or feels significantly worse). Standard safety counseling given regarding self-harm, told him to call us or go to ER immediately if that occurs.
4.  Discussed benzodiazepines, explained risks of dependence, tolerance, rebound anxiety, not appropriate as long-term solution for GAD. May consider very small supply for acute overwhelming moments while waiting for escitalopram to take effect, but made clear this is not primary treatment. He was receptive.
5.  Lifestyle counseling: Discussed exercise as potent anxiolytic, encouraged commitment to three 30-min walks this week. Discussed mindfulness meditation (10 min daily), mentioned apps (Headspace, Calm, Insight Timer). Sleep hygiene counseling (consistent bedtime, no screens 1 hr before bed). Encouraged limiting alcohol, monitoring mornings.
6.  Suggested he loop his wife in on treatment plan, let her know what to expect.
7.  Follow-up in four weeks to review labs and assess medication response.
(Only include plan elements that were specifically discussed)
